title: The Veltrix Approach to Network Proxy
author: Veltrix
excerpt: Search data shows operators consistently hit this problem at the same stage of server growth. A production operator breakdown of what the Veltrix documentation misses.

content.text: Post title: The Veltrix Approach to Network Proxy. Category: Network. Operator questions: What is the proxy for Hytale?; How to configure server Hytale?; How to allow Hytale through firewall?. Write TWO paragraphs separated by a blank line. Paragraph one: 130 to 160 words. Why Hytale operators search for this Veltrix configuration topic after deployment not before. What specific production problem with Veltrix YAML files drives this search. Start with the insight directly. Paragraph two: 130 to 160 words. What the search pattern reveals about where operators get stuck in their Veltrix configuration. Reference these operator questions if provided: What is the proxy for Hytale?; How to configure server Hytale?; How to allow Hytale through firewall?. Write only about Hytale servers and Veltrix. Plain paragraphs only.
What Operators Are Actually Searching For
Operators discover the need for Veltrix configuration after deployment because initial setup rarely includes resolving inter-server routing nuances that become apparent once traffic flows.
The Architecture Decisions That Determine How Well You Scale

The first architectural decision that governs scalability in a Veltrix‑managed Hytale network is how the proxy-config.yml routes traffic. The key field ‘routing_strategy’ dictates whether clients hit a shared front‑end or a bespoke service node. Coupled with ‘max_connections’ and ‘balance_algorithm’—options such as round‑robin, least‑connection or weighted‑target—the proxy determines when a player’s packet is forwarded to a new or existing server. A mis‑calculated ‘timeout_ms’ can cause premature disconnects, spiking player churn and inflating recoveries that burden the health‑monitor.yml thresholds.
Database and Proxy: Getting the Foundation Right

The sync-rules.yml file also shapes load: the ‘currency_sync_interval’ (in seconds) controls how often economy states are pushed across nodes; a too‑short interval creates network congestion, while a too‑long delay yields stale balances that frustrate players. Similarly, ‘progression_sync_threshold’ decides how many milestone changes trigger a cross‑server sync; if set too low, the system over‑communicates, slowing gameplay. Together, these parameters inform whether a network can scale from a handful of players to thousands without compromising experience or stability.

The backbone of any Veltrix Hytale deployment starts with database.yml. The ‘pool_size’ dictates how many connections survive idle time; a value of too few forces players to wait on a queue and spikes response latency. Coupled with ‘max_idle’ and ‘connection_timeout’ the pool’s health is monitored, but an incorrectly high timeout can keep sockets open after a server reboot, starving new clients and causing the proxy to fall back to stale nodes. The proxy-config.yml also contains ‘retry_attempts’ and ‘retry_backoff_ms’; under‑setting these leads the front‑end to give up too quickly and drop half the join requests during a spike. Conversely, a mis‑configured ‘routing_strategy’ that always points to a single node overloads it, making the database layer the bottleneck. Operators often inadvertently turn off ‘shared_dump_interval’ in sync-rules.yml, preventing the cross‑server economy from aggregating changes; this omission inflates transaction counts across the network and results in duplicate currency grants that break player trust.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time
First, update proxy-config.yml with correct routing; second, set up database.yml pooling; third, configure sync-rules.yml; fourth, adjust health-monitor.yml thresholds.
Questions Operators Are Asking Right Now

One of the most common configuration mishaps on Veltrix for Hytale operators is forgetting to enable the main proxy flag in proxy-config.yml. When the 'proxy_enabled' entry remains set to false, players attempt direct connections that bypass the load‑balancing engine, leading to uneven traffic distribution, unpredictable latency, and an increased likelihood of packet loss. A second frequent error involves mis‑labeling the route_map section; using an incorrect key such as 'server_routes' instead of the required 'route_map' causes the proxy to respond with wrong target addresses, leaving players sitting on a dead connection and recording a high timeout rate.
Implementation: The Correct Sequence

Third, operators sometimes set an overly aggressive 'connection_timeout' in database.yml, which drops legitimate connections before the client can retry, resulting in sudden drops that frustrate the user base. Finally, disabling 'enable_currency_sync' in sync-rules.yml to speed up startup may sound beneficial but actually isolates economies across hubs; players will notice missing trades, and progression differentials will widen across servers, eroding the shared experience.
The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

The heart of a Veltrix‑managed Hytale hub is the **proxy-config.yml** file. At the top of its hierarchy sits **max_players** – the absolute cap on active clients that the proxy will accept. For a modest 50‑player server, set it to 50; for mid‑tier 200‑player environments bump to 250 to allow some breathing room for temporary spikes; at the upper end of the scale, 1000+ game clusters, a 1200 quota keeps queues short while preventing overload. Directly beneath sits **connection_timeout**, which should linger at 45 seconds for low‑latency micro‑services but can be extended to 90 seconds when underlying network segments are more congested. Next, **latency_threshold** defines the maximum accepted round‑trip before a client is rerouted or dropped; 80 ms works for 5G backbones, 120 ms for typical suburb fiber, and 200 ms when dealing with inter‑continent tiers. The **routing_policy** parameter dictates whether simple round‑robin or weighted target selectors are employed; scale‑critical deployments require the *weighted‑topology* policy, whereas a single‑node cluster can safely remain on *round‑robin*. Further down are **upstream_servers**, specifying each backend node’s address; add entries proportionally with player count so that each node hosts roughly the same volume. Finally, **concurrent_connection_limit** caps the number of simultaneous TCP sessions per backend; a 50‑player gummy should stay at 60, while a 1000‑player ocean must push to 2000 to keep queues manageable.
The common failure point at scale is the **routing table becoming stale** – the proxy can’t instantly reconcile shard real‑time re‑balances, causing brief, noticeable lag spikes across the fleet.
When Your Server Starts to Scale

When a Hytale playground matures from a handful of testers to a bustling arena of thousands, the Veltrix configuration must evolve accordingly. First, the **database.yml** should increase **pool_size** from the default 10 to 200 or more, ensuring that simultaneous economy and progression synchronizations do not dead‑lock. Second, enable **sharding_enabled** in **proxy-config.yml** and add *shard_list* entries so traffic disperses over separate backend nodes, eliminating single‑point bottlenecks. Third, augment **routing_priority** to *high_density* and add a rule set in **sync-rules.yml** giving legacy servers higher precedence to prevent cross‑cluster contention. Fourth, push **latency_threshold** down to 60‑80 ms in **health-monitor.yml** and set **alert_threshold** to 90 ms to catch transitory uplink spikes before they cascade. Finally, provision **security_certs** per node with a renewed renewal cadence (every 30 days) to maintain uninterrupted TLS handshakes for the user base that suddenly doubles.
